does: This paper demonstrates that standard model-level alignment benchmarks cannot reliably predict how well language models will actually remain aligned when deployed in real-world interactive settings. The authors conduct a systematic audit of eleven alignment benchmarks (extended to sixteen) using a structured eight-dimension rubric, achieving high inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa = 0.87). They find that nearly all examined benchmarks lack user-facing verification support and assess artificial, static scenarios rather than properties like process steerability that matter during actual deployment. The paper establishes a framework distinguishing alignment evidence across four levels: model-level (benchmark scores on fixed inputs), response-level (individual outputs), interaction-level (multi-turn conversations), and deployment-level (real-world usage contexts), arguing that claims about deployed alignment require evidence collected at the deployment level or higher.
connects: This work builds on growing awareness within the alignment community that model capabilities benchmarks often fail to predict downstream behavior—a concern that has driven recent work on emergent misalignment in deployment and the gap between training and deployment. The paper extends prior critiques of narrow model evaluation by focusing specifically on alignment benchmarks and their structural limitations. It engages with existing interactional alternatives like tau-bench, CURATe, Rifts, and Common Ground, positioning them as steps toward deployment-relevant evaluation but fragmented across different dimensions. The research responds to the proliferation of influential alignment benchmarks such as those measuring truthfulness, instruction-following, and preference scores, which have become standard in claims about deployed alignment safety.
enables: This work opens pathways toward deployment-centric alignment evaluation methodologies. It points toward building more comprehensive benchmarks that measure multi-turn interaction dynamics, user-model verification loops, and process steerability rather than static model outputs. The framework for indexing alignment claims to evidence level provides a foundation for distinguishing between what model evaluation can legitimately claim versus what requires interaction or deployment-level evidence. This could enable more honest communication about alignment assurance in practice, directing future benchmark construction toward scenarios that genuinely predict real-world deployment behavior. The paper also sets up research into how to design efficient deployment-level evaluations and what minimal interactional properties best predict downstream alignment in practice.
